meta.title: Lev Architecture Tag Soup
meta.description: A long-form architecture taxonomy page covering Compiled Declaration Architecture, the compiled-declaration spine, family comparisons, epistemology tags, subsystem decompilation, and the session convergence.

props.title: Compiled Declaration Architecture, Without Architecture Slop
props.subtitle: One long-form super JSON for the family, the spine, the tag soup, and the session convergence.
props.meta: April 2026 · GPT compare + repo sweep + CDO convergence

props.content: The session converged on one core answer: Lev does have a recurring compiler-shaped architecture, but it is not the universal ontology of the repo. The right framing is a **Compiled Declaration Architecture** applied to a **compiled-declaration spine**. That spine is strongest in FlowMind, Orchestration, Poly, and likely LevUI. Everything else should be classified honestly through planes, families, fit classes, and failure classes rather than being forced into the same mold.

props.label: CONVERGENCE
props.title: GPT and CDO landed in the same neighborhood
props.subtitle: The overlap is high. The remaining delta is mostly about scope and naming discipline.

props.title: 🤖 GPT synthesis
props.content: GPT got remarkably close: declaration -> compiler/lowering -> stable IR -> runtime/projection -> optional federation, four runtime planes, authored roots as truth, compiled view as frozen meaning, and runtime as proof-emitting state machine. The main overreach was treating the family slightly too broadly and using substrate language where the repo already reserves deeper substrate meanings.

props.title: 🧠 CDO synthesis
props.content: CDO forced the edge cases: logger, LevEvent, projections, federation, YAML-as-projection, and root DNA scope. It converged on **Compiled Declaration Architecture** as the external family name, **compiled-declaration spine** as the internal name, and an optional DNA block instead of a new universal root doctrine.

props.title: ✅ Settled
props.content: Use `Compiled Declaration Architecture` for the family. Use `compiled-declaration spine` for the scoped Lev-native application. Keep `lens` language only for governance and fit decisions.

props.label: FAMILY MATRIX
props.title: Architecture families, runtime families, and implementation patterns
props.subtitle: This is the decomposition pass: clean architecture, hexagonal, compiler architecture, model-driven systems, event systems, runtimes, and implementation patterns all get classified against the same epistemic questions.

rows.stable: compiled IR or stable handoff form
rows.warning: can become fake-IR architecture slop if universalized

rows.stable: often implicit, not always a named IR
rows.warning: captures boundaries, but misses declaration-to-IR spine
too generic for Lev's compiler-specific shape
classic compiler framing can overfit and ignore projections and federation
can become too tooling-heavy or too codegen-centric
undersells pre-runtime admission and compiled contract boundaries
not part of the compiled-declaration spine
runtime portability says little about authored truth or repo-wide ontology
too low-level to explain Lev's multi-surface semantic contracts
useful locally, not an architectural family

rows.transform: entry adapters -> normalized fractal root -> cascade merge
rows.warning: is authoring/config architecture, not the whole compiled-declaration spine by itself

props.content: The key takeaway is that Lev is not inventing an entirely new architecture family out of thin air. It is combining established families in a very consistent way. The distinctive move is not novelty for novelty’s sake; it is that compiled declarations and stable handoff forms keep surfaces from improvising meaning.

props.label: TAG SOUP
props.title: The actual taxonomy we should use
props.subtitle: One family name is not enough. We need orthogonal tags so subsystems can be decompiled honestly.

props.caption: Orthogonal tags, not one universal label
props.content:
flowchart TD
  A[Subsystem] --> B[Plane]
  A --> C[Family]
  A --> D[Fit Class]
  A --> E[Layer Presence]
  A --> F[Failure Class]

  B --> B1[control]
  B --> B2[execution]
  B --> B3[state]
  B --> B4[causality]
  B --> B5[projection]
  B --> B6[observability]

  C --> C1[compiled_declaration_architecture]
  C --> C2[lev_event_spine]
  C --> C3[observability_leaf]
  C --> C4[shared_contracts_only]
  C --> C5[projection_only]
  C --> C6[adapter_runtime]

  D --> D1[strong]
  D --> D2[partial]
  D --> D3[non_fit]

  E --> E1[declaration]
  E --> E2[lowering]
  E --> E3[stable_form]
  E --> E4[runtime]
  E --> E5[federation]

  F --> F1[gap]
  F --> F2[drift]
  F --> F3[misalignment]

meta_substrate:
  fit_class: strong          # strong | partial | non_fit
  declaration_ref: null
  lowering_ref: null
  stable_form:
    kind: none               # graph_document | registry_runtime | ui_ir | typed_envelope | none
    ref: null
  runtime_ref: null
  non_fit_reason: null       # observability_leaf | lev_event_spine | shared_contracts_only | projection_only | no_compiler_seam
  orthogonal_contracts: []   # e.g. [lev_event_spine, observability]

failure_classes:
  gap:
    - declaration_missing
    - lowering_missing
    - stable_form_missing
    - runtime_owner_missing
  drift:
    - runtime_bypasses_stable_form
    - owner_boundary_violation
    - duplicate_canonical_contract
    - shadow_ir
  misalignment:
    - projection_confusion
    - fake_stable_form
    - lev_event_spine_confusion
    - logger_forcing

props.label: DECOMPILER
props.title: Decompile the repo honestly
props.subtitle: This is the large matrix of epistemology: what kind of thing each subsystem actually is, what plane it lives in, what family it belongs to, and how it fails.

rows.notes: consumes compiled intent; does not author flow YAML
concept converged; repo still has binder drift
state plane, not the whole ontology
root causality plane; do not coerce into compiled-declaration family
supporting architecture, not the whole spine

props.title: ⚠️ Partial is not weak
props.content: Partial means the subsystem has a runtime and some real structure, but not the full authored-declaration-to-stable-form chain under one honest owner. Runtime is nearly universal. Compiled-declaration is selective.

props.label: SESSION
props.title: How the session converged
props.subtitle: From Poly/FlowMind intuition to a scoped architectural family with a contract sketch.

items.title: Pattern spotted in Poly and FlowMind
items.description: The declaration -> compiler -> IR -> runtime shape showed up strongly enough to test across the rest of the repo.

items.title: Cross-repo sweep
items.description: Core, plugins, and specs were sampled. Strong in the FlowMind/Orchestration/Poly/Graph/UI spine. Partial and non-fits became explicit.

items.title: Meta-substrate doctrine saved
items.description: The first doctrine note was written, but scope and naming were still too fuzzy.

items.title: CDO taxonomy pass
items.description: Architecture vs lens vs substrate vs topology was tested against logger, LevEvent, federation, and the actual spine.

items.title: Convergence
items.description: The family name settled on Compiled Declaration Architecture, scoped to the compiled-declaration spine, with lens retained as the governance guardrail.

props.title: 🚀 Immediate next actions
props.content: 1. Patch the canonical meta-substrate design note with the converged naming and contract. 2. Add an optional `meta_substrate` block to the strong-fit spine only. 3. Return to the Poly implementation slice: protocol nouns, compiler seam, fixture goldens, CLI proof, Stack-backed runtime proof.
